text.title: Decomposition Principles and Size Rules
text.summary: Concrete file-size thresholds, token-budget planning, and the when-to-split checklist for LLM-friendly code structure.

rule.statement: Each file must have one clear purpose; if you cannot name the file without using "and" or "utils", split it.
rule.rationale: Multi-purpose files grow indefinitely; single-purpose files have a natural size ceiling.

example.note: Django users module split by layer: models.py, serializers.py, views.py, permissions.py, services.py — each under 200 lines.
example.code: users/ models.py # Data structure only serializers.py # API representation views.py # HTTP handlers permissions.py # Access control services.py # Business logic tests/ test_models.py test_views.py test_services.py

rule.statement: Split any file that exceeds its type's max-lines threshold; target the ideal range to start.
rule.rationale: Files beyond ~300 lines require excessive scrolling for humans and exceed effective LLM context when combined with tests.

list.item: Model/Entity: ideal 50-100 lines, max 200 — split by domain
Service: ideal 100-200 lines, max 300 — extract sub-services
View/Controller: ideal 50-150 lines, max 250 — extract handlers
React Component: ideal 50-150 lines, max 250 — extract sub-components
Test file: ideal 100-300 lines, max 500 — split by scenario
Config: ideal 50-100 lines, max 150 — split by environment

section.title: Token Budget Planning

rule.statement: If a single source file exceeds 500 lines (~15k tokens), split it before scheduling LLM-assisted edits.
rule.rationale: Effective LLM context is far smaller than nominal; a 500-line file plus its tests and task context saturates a 50k-token window.

section.p: 100k-token budget allocation: task context ~5k, existing code to read ~20k, generation ~30k, testing ~20k, buffer ~25k.

list.item: Multiple classes or components in one file
Hard to test (need to mock too much)
LLM produces incomplete or wrong diffs for the file
File is under 100 lines and tightly cohesive
Splitting would only add indirection without context savings
Would break encapsulation by exposing internals
